facts: Light is an electromagnetic wave, not a mechanical wave like sound, so it can travel through the vacuum of space without needing a medium.

The electromagnetic spectrum includes radio, microwave, infrared, visible, ultraviolet, X-ray, and gamma waves, all differing in wavelength and frequency; visible light is only a narrow band between infrared and ultraviolet.
Infrared waves have longer wavelengths than visible light; they are emitted by warm objects and can be felt as heat, but they are not the same as the thermal motion of molecules.
Ultraviolet waves have shorter wavelengths than visible light and carry more energy, which is why they can damage skin cells and cause sunburn even on cloudy days.
Refraction occurs when light changes speed as it moves from one material to another, causing it to bend; for example, a straw in a glass of water appears bent because light slows down in water.
Reflection happens when light bounces off a surface; a mirror reflects most visible light because its smooth surface causes regular reflection, while rough surfaces scatter light.
misconceptions: Students believe that radio waves, microwaves, and X-rays are completely different from 'light' and are not electromagnetic waves, when in fact they are all part of the same electromagnetic spectrum.
Students think that light travels faster in water than in air because water is denser, but light actually slows down in water, causing the bending we call refraction.

Students assume that ultraviolet light is just a type of heat or that it only causes damage when it is bright, not recognizing that UV carries higher energy than visible light and can harm skin and eyes even in indirect sunlight.
